blocks.content: She lay back on the bed and played it a third time, and by the third time she knew what she was going to do.

There was a particular quality to it — the part that kept coming back, the lyric about what someone brings out in you. The version of yourself you only find in the company of certain people. Not wild like reckless. Wild like alive — like unguarded, like all the parts of yourself that don't get let out in ordinary circumstances. You bring out the wild side.
That was what he did. She hadn't had the right word for it before tonight. She had it now.

She sat up. She pulled up their conversation. She typed and deleted once — second-guessed herself for exactly three seconds, which was less time than she'd spent second-guessing anything else today — and then she sent it.

She put the phone on her chest and looked at the ceiling. Her heart was doing something loud. She was aware that sending it was not the same as telling him the truth, but it was adjacent to it. It was reaching through the space between them with something real. It was the version of herself that existed with him saying: I see you. I feel this.
